food_name: Otah Otah (Grilled Spiced Mackerel Fish Cake in Banana Leaf)
aliases: grilled fish paste in banana leaf

english: grilled fish paste in banana leaf

food_subgroup: Grilled minced mackerel fish paste with coconut milk and rempah (lemongrass, galangal, chilli, turmeric, belacan), wrapped and grilled in banana leaf

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Otah Otah. No exact URI can be assigned without fabrication. Closest conceptual parent is a spiced fish cake preparation. URI left null per ontology honesty policy.
ontology.foodex2_description: Singapore grilled spiced mackerel fish paste in banana leaf (otah otah) — mackerel, coconut milk, rempah (lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, chilli, belacan), egg; closest_parent match
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0BDF is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a processed fish product. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for otah otah as a named dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard otah otah piece is a banana-leaf parcel containing approximately 80 g of grilled spiced mackerel fish paste, equivalent to roughly 130 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Otah Otah — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Otah Otah (grilled spiced mackerel fish cake in banana leaf). HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. All per-100g values are taken from this verified entry. Micronutrient values for selenium, vitamin D, and phosphorus are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Atlantic mackerel (NDB 15046) to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Fish-Based Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for otah otah based on protein-dominant spiced fish cake profile
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for otah otah exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 42 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) protein-dominant mackerel fish paste composition (14.5g protein, 5.2g carbohydrate per 100g); (2) published GI range for spiced fish cakes and fish paste products (~40–52) from the University of Sydney GI Database; (3) low carbohydrate content (5.2g/100g) and presence of dietary fibre (0.8g) and fat (8.8g) which blunt glycemic response. GL per 80g serving = (42 × 4.16g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.7. This is a low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 480 mg per 100 g from belacan (fermented shrimp paste) and fish seasoning
hypertension.guidance: An 80 g serving delivers approximately 384 mg sodium — about 17% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — which is significant for a small snack item; individuals with hypertension should limit otah otah to one piece per meal and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes such as sambal or salted fish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~42) and very low glycemic load per serving (GL 1.7) due to protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate composition
type2_diabetes.guidance: Otah otah is an excellent protein accompaniment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: GI ~42 and GL 1.7 per 80 g piece produce minimal postprandial glucose excursion, and the mackerel omega-3 content supports insulin sensitivity; it is best paired with a moderate portion of low-GI rice or vegetables rather than white bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 DHA/EPA from Spanish mackerel (tenggiri), with moderate saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g) from coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids from mackerel (estimated 0.8–1.2 g DHA/EPA per 80 g serving) provide clinically meaningful cardioprotective benefit, reducing triglycerides and platelet aggregation; saturated fat from coconut milk (4.5 g/100 g) is moderate and acceptable within a heart-healthy diet when otah otah is consumed 2–3 times per week rather than daily.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 DHA/EPA, selenium (38 mcg/100 g), vitamin D (3.5 mcg/100 g), and curcumin from turmeric in rempah
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Otah otah is one of the most anti-inflammatory items in Singapore's hawker landscape: mackerel omega-3 suppresses pro-inflammatory eicosanoids, selenium (38 mcg/100 g) supports antioxidant glutathione peroxidase activity, vitamin D (3.5 mcg/100 g) is clinically relevant for RA bone protection, and curcumin from turmeric in the rempah is the most studied natural anti-inflammatory phytochemical.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Otah Otah (also spelled otak-otak, literally meaning 'brains' in Malay — likely a reference to its soft, wobbly, brain-like texture) is one of Singapore's most iconic and sensorial hawker street snacks, with deep roots in Malay and Peranakan culinary tradition shared across Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia. The Singapore version is made from minced Spanish mackerel (ikan tenggiri) blended with rich coconut milk and a freshly pounded rempah — a fragrant spice paste of lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, dried chillies, shallots, garlic, and belacan (fermented shrimp paste) — combined with egg to bind the mixture. The paste is spooned onto a fresh banana leaf, which is folded into a neat rectangular parcel and secured, then grilled over charcoal. During grilling, the banana leaf chars and steams simultaneously, imparting a subtle smoky, grassy, green fragrance to the fish paste inside. The result is a softly set, vivid orange-red fish cake that is simultaneously spicy, sweet, coconutty, oceanic, and herbal — one of the most complex flavour profiles in Singapore street food. Nutritionally, otah otah is a powerhouse: at 162 kcal per 100 g with 14.5 g protein, it is protein-dense and low in carbohydrates (5.2 g/100 g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 42 and a glycemic load of only 1.7 per 80 g serving. It is exceptionally rich in selenium (38 mcg/100 g), vitamin D (3.5 mcg/100 g), vitamin B12 (2.5 mcg/100 g), and niacin (5.5 mg/100 g) from mackerel, and contains curcumin from turmeric and anti-inflammatory phytochemicals from galangal and lemongrass in the rempah. The main nutritional caution is its sodium content (480 mg/100 g) from belacan and fish seasoning. Otah otah is sold at hawker centres, Malay food stalls, and kopitiams across Singapore, typically as a side dish with white rice or as a standalone snack.
common_user_queries: is otah otah good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. HPB is a government health authority database with verified nutritional data. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. Multilingual naming is complete for all mandatory Singaporean dish languages.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for otah otah (grilled spiced mackerel fish paste) is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; mackerel is known to contain significant omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (DHA/EPA) but the exact per-100g values for this prepared dish are not available in the approved source as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this prepared dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional otah otah preparation). GI of 42 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for otah otah exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sodium at 480 mg/100 g is primarily from belacan (fermented shrimp paste) — individuals on sodium-restricted diets should be cautious. Two portions in the input had is_default: true for 80g — the 80g banana leaf parcel is used as the canonical serving size. The 40g portion (grabfood source) is noted but grabfood is a forbidden source per Project Veritas policy; the 80g HPB-verified portion is used as the canonical default.
